visits.text: Faro Cidade Velha: Moorish ramparts still containing a real lived-in old town, not an open-air museum. The Sé cathedral (13th century, rebuilt after the 1755 earthquake) merits the bell tower climb to see the lagoon stretching to the horizon. The municipal museum in the Nossa Senhora da Assunção convent displays Roman mosaics from Ossonoba.

Storks have nested in the Arco da Vila towers ever since they chose these battlements as a breeding site. The neoclassical arch dates from 1812, commissioned by Bishop Francisco Gomes do Avelar. It marks the entrance to the fortified city from the Jardim Manuel Bívar. Below, the old Moorish gate from the 11th century—one of Portugal's oldest preserved horseshoe arches.

Sixty kilometers of lagoons, sandbanks, and barrier islands—the Ria Formosa stretches from Faro to Cacela Velha. A protected natural park, one of Europe's most important for migratory birds. Ilha Deserta, accessible by boat from the port, is genuinely deserted: an Atlantic beach in its raw state.

Built on the ruins of a Moorish medina, Faro's Cidade Velha can be explored in under an hour—just enough to soak up the Iberian atmosphere. The Largo da Sé with its orange trees and the cathedral with its 18th-century azulejos form a human-scale historic center, crowd-free before 10 a.m. Free access to the square.

The scent of warm bread and orange blossoms fills Rua de Santo António from early morning—it's the main shopping street, pleasant for a coffee before hitting the road. Understated, not spectacular, but honest.

activities.text: If you have an afternoon, take the boat to Ilha Deserta from the Porta Nova pier. The crossing takes 20 minutes. On the island: a beach stretching several kilometers, one restaurant at the end, the Atlantic on the other side of the dunes. Nothing else. The return with the sunset behind Faro is worth the trip.

Ilha Deserta: there's genuinely nothing—that's the point. White sand, turquoise water, the Atlantic horizon without infrastructure. The restaurant at the island's end serves fresh seafood at reasonable prices. Arrive early to get a terrace seat before the Algarve day-trippers invade.
Pick up your rental car at the airport and head straight north on the N122. The Serra do Caldeirão gives way to the arid hills of the Baixo Alentejo. Budget 1 hour 45 minutes to Mértola.

days_plan.itin_title: Huelva, the Forgotten Land: Doñana to Faro in 7 Days
Border Alentejo: Guadiana and Alqueva in 7 Days

visits.text: Discover Albufeira, the most popular tourist resort in southern Portugal offering something for every type of traveler. From scenic coastlines and long sandy beaches to a charming old town with traditional architecture, this central Algarve hub provides excellent access to attractions throughout the region. The old fishermen's neighborhood and clifftop old town are particularly atmospheric areas to explore.

Visit Praia da Falésia, one of the world's most unique beaches stretching over 6 kilometers between Vilamoura and Albufeira. The impressive red sandstone cliffs change colors depending on weather and light, creating breathtaking scenery worth visiting in any season. The nicest stretch near Olhos de Agua features particularly dramatic cliff formations that photograph beautifully.

Explore the picturesque Chapel of Nossa Senhora da Rocha in Porches, sitting dramatically on coastal cliffs above the ocean. While the little chapel itself is charming, the main attraction is the spectacular coastal scenery visible from paths leading westward. This popular sunset location rewards visitors with stunning views of the Algarve coastline.

activities.text: Experience kayaking from St Rafael Beach in Albufeira, paddling along the beautiful coastline to discover hidden caves and secluded coves. Water sports enthusiasts will find parasailing, jet skiing, and countless other activities available throughout the summer season. The beaches around Albufeira like Praia dos Arrifes and Praia São Rafael offer some of the most scenic coastal swimming in central Algarve.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal Grand Tour: Algarve Coast to Northern Mountain Peaks

places.name: Alcacer Do Sal

visits.text: Alcácer do Sal comes from Arabic Al-Qasr - the fortress - and sal for the salt pans that made its fortune since Antiquity. The castle atop the hill was conquered by Afonso Henriques in 1158, then taken and retaken until 1217. Now converted into a pousada, the keep and ramparts remain visitable. Clear view over the Sado estuary and rice fields.

The historic center of Alcácer descends gently from the castle to the river. White houses with blue or yellow trim, several baroque churches, an archaeology museum installed in the archaeological crypt of the Pelourinho. Natural lunch stop on the route between Beja and Lisbon.

activities.text: Lunch on enguias fritas (fried eels) or açorda de sável (shad soup with garlic and coriander) in a riverside tasca. Local specialties linked to the river, to taste in the cool season (October-March).

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Lisbon and Southern Portugal Tour in 15 Days

map_keywords: Castelo de Alcácer do Sal Pousada
Centro Histórico Alcácer do Sal Sado

visits.text: The Moorish castle of Aljezur fell in 1249, among the last Moorish strongholds conquered in Portugal - the Reconquista ended here very late. The ruins dominate the valley from a basalt peak. From the summit, view over the inland valley and the Atlantic in the distance. Free climb, path marked from the main square.

The Museu Municipal of Aljezur is tiny - but the local Reconquista is carefully recounted there, with Islamic ceramics and campaign maps to support it. This is where you understand why this coast resisted longer than the rest of the country. Ticket a few euros, visit in 45 minutes.

activities.text: Walk the castle trail on foot: from Praça da Liberdade, the path climbs in 10 minutes to the ramparts. View over the Aljezur valley and the Bordeira dunes in the distance. Descent through the alleyways of the historic quarter.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Lisbon and Southern Portugal Tour in 15 Days

visits.text: The star fortress of Almeida is unique: 12 points, a plan inspired by Vauban but adapted to the local topography, built in the 17th and 18th centuries. The circuit of the bastions on foot runs 4 km and takes 2 hours. Better preserved than Elvas (UNESCO-listed), less known—tour buses don't come here yet.

On August 26, 1810, a French shell landed in the fortress courtyard, ignited a powder trail left by a poorly closed barrel, and triggered a chain explosion of the main depot. Hundreds of defenders killed in seconds. The town capitulated the next day. The crater is still visible today.

activities.text: Walk the bastion circuit in the morning—4 km, 2 hours, free entry. The entire walking route follows the outer ramparts and passes through all the star bastions. Bring water: no café on the circuit.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal: Historic Villages & Serra da Estrela in 8 Days

places.name: Alter Do Chao

visits.text: Sixty mares come out to the field every morning at the Coudelaria de Alter Real: that's the moment to see. This royal stud farm founded in 1748 by King João V to supply haute école horses to the Lisbon court is one of the world's oldest stud farms in continuous operation on its original site. 800 hectares, pure Lusitano breed.

The Coudelaria de Alter Real stud farm was founded in 1748 by King João V, who purchased the first mares of the strain in Andalusia. The on-site museum tells the history of this breed and displays saddles, bits, and harnesses from the 16th century. Remarkable collection of baroque equipment.

activities.text: Depending on the season, academic riding demonstrations take place at the Coudelaria—inquire at the entrance. Outside demonstrations, you can watch the stallions training in the covered riding halls. Calling ahead recommended before making the detour: +351 245 619 060.

days_plan.itin_title: Lisbon and Upper Alentejo Fortresses in 8 Days

map_keywords: Alter Real Stud Farm Alter do Chão

visits.text: Founded probably at the turn of the Christian era, the Roman city of Ammaia was identified and excavated only from 1994–1995. The site covers twenty original hectares, of which only a tiny fraction is uncovered: forum, baths, south gate. Classified as a National Monument since 1949, it lies in the same municipality of São Salvador da Aramenha as Marvão. Entrance €3, open 9 AM–12:30 PM and 2–5:30 PM.

Small museum, big surprise: the Roman glassware collection from Ammaia at Quinta do Deão is one of the most remarkable on the Iberian Peninsula. Cups, flasks, unguentaria—nearly intact forms after two thousand years. The staff speaks English and explains the context enthusiastically.

activities.text: Walk to the south gate to understand the extent of what was once a city of several thousand inhabitants—far more populated than Marvão is today. The contrast between the forgotten Roman city below and the visible medieval village on the ridge to the north tells everything about the shift of civilizations.

days_plan.itin_title: Lisbon and Upper Alentejo Fortresses in 8 Days

visits.text: Explore the Arouca UNESCO Global Geopark, a protected landscape renowned for extraordinary geological heritage including the famous Giant Trilobites of Canelas. This territory surrounded by the Freita, Montemuro, and Arada mountain ranges represents millions of years of Earth's history preserved in remarkable rock formations. The geopark offers adventure activities amid dramatic natural scenery.

Walk the Passadiços do Paiva, a spectacular 8-kilometer wooden boardwalk zigzagging along the Paiva River gorge. This engineering marvel passes thundering waterfalls, sheer cliffs, and pristine swimming holes carved into bedrock. The walkways provide safe access to one of Portugal's most dramatic river landscapes previously inaccessible to visitors.

activities.text: Cross the 516 Arouca, one of the longest pedestrian suspension bridges in the world, stretching 516 meters across the Paiva River gorge at 175 meters height. This thrilling walkway offers breathtaking views of the valley far below and surrounding mountain landscape. Advance tickets are required for this unforgettable adventure experience.

Challenge yourself with white water rafting on the Paiva River, considered one of Portugal's premier destinations for river sports. Rapids range from gentle to demanding, with professional guides leading expeditions through spectacular gorge scenery. Kayaking and canyoning adventures provide different ways to experience this remarkable waterway.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal Grand Tour: Algarve Coast to Northern Mountain Peaks
Portugal North Bound: Lisbon to Peneda-Gerês National Park

visits.text: Visit the unique Circular Castle of Arraiolos, a defensive structure that is unique in Portugal for its rounded shape. From atop its walls, enjoy spectacular views over the green landscapes of the Alentejo and the historic village below.

Explore the Arraiolos Carpet Interpretive Center, an interactive museum that tells the story of this centuries-old art considered one of Portugal's first crafts. Arraiolos carpets are world-famous, and the town breathes this tradition on every corner.

Wander through the historic center of Arraiolos, discovering the Arraiolos Carpet Square, the central plaza with cafés and craft shops. The village preserves its picturesque character with white houses and cobblestone streets typical of the Alentejo.

activities.text: Visit the Monte da Ravasqueira winery, which offers wine tourism experiences including tastings and a visit to the Private Carriage Collection. Discover the stories behind the wines of this prestigious Alentejo winery.

days_plan.itin_title: Alentejo UNESCO Heritage: Évora, Elvas and Medieval Villages
Inland Alentejo to the Wild Vicentine Coast

visits.text: Embark on a traditional moliceiro boat ride through Aveiro's picturesque canals, often dubbed the Venice of Portugal. These colorful vessels, once used for seaweed harvesting, now glide past stunning Art Nouveau facades and charming bridges. Each boat features vibrant paintings depicting local humor and maritime traditions, offering a unique window into the city's cultural heritage.

Explore the Aveiro Museum housed in the former Convento de Jesus, where Princess Saint Joana spent her final years. This remarkable collection showcases sacred art spanning centuries, including the ornate baroque tomb of the princess herself. The museum building alone, with its gilded woodwork and azulejo tiles, represents a masterpiece of Portuguese religious architecture.

Wander through Aveiro's Art Nouveau quarter, a treasure trove of early 20th-century architecture centered around the former ceramics factory. Marvel at the elaborately decorated facades featuring the iconic Portuguese azulejo tiles, intricate ironwork balconies, and organic motifs that define this distinctive architectural style.

activities.text: Paddle through the Ria de Aveiro by kayak, discovering the vast lagoon ecosystem with its salt marshes, diverse birdlife, and traditional fishing villages. This protected wetland offers a peaceful escape into nature just minutes from the city center.

Visit the Écomusée Marinha da Troncalhada to discover the ancient art of salt harvesting that shaped Aveiro's economy for centuries. Watch as workers use traditional techniques to extract salt from the crystalline ponds, continuing a practice dating back to Roman times.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal Discovery Tour: From Lisbon to Alentejo Plains

visits.text: Nobody comes to Beja on purpose. That's precisely what makes it interesting. A discreet district capital, Roman (Pax Julia), Visigothic, Moorish, it has survived everything without ever becoming famous. The Praça da República at the center, lined with whitewashed houses with colorful trim, is one of the finest squares in the Alentejo without appearing on any guidebook cover.

The keep of the Castelo de Beja rises 40 meters—the Torre de Menagem, built entirely of marble, features an eight-pointed star vault at the summit, characteristic of the region's medieval military architecture. From the battlements, views stretch endlessly across the Alentejo plains. Small admission fee.

A 15th-century convent houses Portugal's most famous love correspondence. The Museu Rainha Dona Leonor, installed in the former Convento da Conceição founded in 1459, is where the nun Mariana Alcoforado allegedly wrote her five letters to a French officer in 1666—published in Paris in 1669 as Lettres portugaises. Real or fake, the debate has never been settled. Check hours before visiting: the main convent is sometimes closed for renovations.

activities.text: Settle in at the Praça da República and order a plate of migas alentejanas or carne de porco à alentejana at one of the restaurants around the square. Beja's cuisine comes from a town that feeds on its own fields: no frills, direct flavors.

The town of Beja breathes Alentejo gastronomy right into its bakeries: local sweets made with nata (cream) and almonds can be found in the downtown pastelarias. A late-afternoon stop for coffee and bolo de mel beats any restaurant dessert.

days_plan.itin_title: Inland Alentejo to the Wild Vicentine Coast
Border Alentejo: Guadiana and Alqueva in 7 Days
The Grand Lisbon and Southern Portugal Tour in 15 Days

visits.text: Too beautiful to be true, yet everything is authentic: the Mosteiro dos Jerónimos is a masterpiece of Manueline style, financed by a 5% tax on the spice trade with India. Built from 1501 onwards by order of Manuel I, it houses Vasco da Gama's tomb. UNESCO-listed since 1983 along with the Torre de Belém. Closed Mondays.

Standing on the Tagus since 1519, the Torre de Belém looks like a stone wedding cake. This Manueline river defense tower, built by Francisco de Arruda between 1514 and 1519, guarded the harbor entrance. A rhinoceros sculpture on the west façade recalls that a living rhino was gifted to King Manuel I - the animal drowned in a shipwreck before reaching Rome. Long queues in summer, arrive early.

Cotton was spun here in 1846, since 2008 it's been home to designers and restaurants: LX Factory occupies 23,000 m² of former factories belonging to the Companhia de Fiação e Tecidos Lisbonense, under the 25 de Abril bridge. The Ler Devagar bookstore, in a former printing house, has shelves reaching to the ceiling and a bicycle suspended in mid-air. Sunday morning flea market.

activities.text: Only a few master pastry chefs know the original recipe for Pastéis de Belém - kept secret since 1837 when monks from the neighboring monastery started selling their tarts to survive after the closure of convents. The Fábrica de Pastéis de Belém, Rua de Belém 84-92, is the only one authorized to use this name. Long queue, but it moves quickly. Order them sprinkled with cinnamon.

Finish with the Padrão dos Descobrimentos in late afternoon: 52 meters of white concrete shaped like a ship's prow, inaugurated in 1960. Controversial, kitsch for some, but the view from the top over the Tagus and the Jerónimos justifies the €4.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Lisbon and Southern Portugal Tour in 15 Days

visits.text: The story of Belmonte's crypto-Jews is barely believable and perfectly documented. Expelled in 1496, they practiced Judaism in secret for over four centuries—no synagogue, no books, by oral transmission, mainly through the women. Revealed in 1917 to a passing Polish engineer. The Museu Judaico de Belmonte, opened in 2005, has received some 400,000 visitors in twenty years. Closed Mondays.

Belmonte Castle stands on the Morro da Esperança at 650 m altitude. Pedro Álvares Cabral grew up here—he was the son of the castle's alcaide, born in Belmonte around 1467. He reached the coast of Brazil on April 22, 1500, commanding a fleet of 13 ships sent toward the Indies by King Manuel I.
The Museu dos Descobrimentos is disconcertingly modern for this village: 16 interactive rooms on Portuguese expansion and Cabral's legacy in Brazil. Much more immersive than expected. Allow 45 min.

activities.text: Allow 1h30 for the Jewish Museum alone—that's the minimum to do the permanent exhibition without rushing. If you're coming with family, the witness videos are subtitled.

Climb to the viewpoint above the castle to see the Cova da Beira—the valley wedged between the Serra da Estrela and the Serra da Gardunha. The cherry orchards below bloom in March-April; in May-June, the harvest draws visitors from across the district.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal: Historic Villages & Serra da Estrela in 8 Days

visits.text: Discover the majestic Bragança Castle, a medieval fortress that has dominated the city since the 12th century. This imposing citadel with its Keep Tower offers exceptional panoramas over the old town rooftops and the surrounding mountains of Montesinho Natural Park. Don't miss the Domus Municipalis, a rare example of Romanesque civil architecture in Portugal.

Immerse yourself in history at the Iberian Museum of Mask and Costume, housed within the citadel walls. This fascinating museum presents the ancestral traditions of Trás-os-Montes Carnival festivals with their impressive ritual masks. Discover how these pagan ceremonies have survived the centuries in this remote region of Portugal.

Explore Montesinho Natural Park, one of Portugal's best-kept secrets. This UNESCO Biosphere Reserve is home to exceptional biodiversity including Iberian wolves, golden eagles, and preserved flora. The landscapes of mountains, oak forests, and traditional schist villages transport you to a forgotten Portugal.

Visit the authentic village of Rio de Onor, a unique geographical curiosity where the Spanish-Portuguese border runs through the houses. The inhabitants still live according to ancestral communal traditions, sharing lands and herds. It's a living testimony to a rural way of life that has virtually disappeared elsewhere in Europe.

activities.text: Stroll through the cobblestone streets of Bragança's historic center, where every stone tells the story of a city that was once the capital of a powerful duchy. Admire the noble facades, baroque churches, and let yourself be charmed by the authentic atmosphere of this border town that has preserved its medieval character intact.

Stop at Gimonde, a charming village crossed by the Onor River where a medieval bridge spans the crystal-clear waters. This picturesque hamlet is the ideal starting point for hikes in the natural park and offers total immersion in Trás-os-Montes rural life with its traditional houses and peaceful atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal's Trás-os-Montes: Bragança, Douro Valley & Montesinho Park
Portugal's Trás-os-Montes: Bragança, Douro Valley & Montesinho Park

visits.text: Sailors called this cape the "end of the world" - the last land before the open Atlantic, America beyond. The Cabo de São Vicente lighthouse entered service in 1846, built on the site of a convent destroyed by the corsair Francis Drake in 1587. Its current range exceeds 60 km. Free parking, free access to the surroundings.

The current lighthouse is one of Europe's most powerful. It stands on the ruins of a 16th-century fortress built to protect the Franciscan convent that stood there - destroyed in 1587 by Drake, rebuilt in 1606. A small museum inside traces the site's history. Open 10am to 5pm-6pm depending on season, adult ticket €1.50.

activities.text: Arrive in late afternoon for the Atlantic sunset: the raking light on the 75-meter black cliffs is the simplest and most effective spectacle on the entire coast. Dozens of people converge here every evening - even in low season.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Lisbon and Southern Portugal Tour in 15 Days

places.name: Cabo Espichel

visits.text: The dinosaur footprints at Cabo Espichel weren't falsified by local devotion - they're perfectly real, dated to the Lower Cretaceous, about 145 million years ago. Several series of sauropod (giant herbivore) tracks are visible on the tilted slabs of Praia dos Lagosteiros and Baía dos Lagosteiros. The legend of Our Lady of the Cape, who allegedly appeared on the back of a mule, arose from medieval interpretation of these giant traces.

The Ermida da Memória, a small chapel of medieval origin, marks the exact spot of the Marian apparition that gave birth to the cult of Nossa Senhora do Cabo Espichel. The current sanctuary - commissioned by Dom Pedro II at the turn of the 18th century - has been under restoration via the Portuguese tourism Revive program since 2014.

activities.text: Walk to the northern tip of the cape to see the Baía dos Lagosteiros from above - 100-meter vertical cliffs dropping into a cove inaccessible by land. The wind here is constant and often violent. The trails near the edge aren't marked, keep your distance.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Lisbon and Southern Portugal Tour in 15 Days

map_keywords: Santuário de Nossa Senhora do Cabo Espichel

visits.text: White storks nest twenty meters above the waves at Cabo Sardão - impassive beneath the gulls' cries, beaks turned seaward. Storks nesting on cliffs above the ocean: an extremely rare phenomenon, documented here since the 1980s. The nesting season runs from April to late August. Free access, parking at Cavaleiro village.

The Cabo Sardão lighthouse entered service in 1915: 17-meter square tower, built backwards according to local tradition - the entrance door faces the sea rather than the land. Its current range is 42 km. It's the westernmost point of the Alentejo coast.

activities.text: Park at Cavaleiro village and walk 20 minutes on the coastal trail to the lighthouse. The path follows the black schist cliffs and passes several stork nests visible to the naked eye. Bring binoculars in May - the chicks are in the nests.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Lisbon and Southern Portugal Tour in 15 Days

visits.text: Praia da Bordeira smells of salt and dried seaweed long before you see it. A freshwater lagoon borders the north of the beach, where the Bordeira river meets the ocean - fine sand dunes, lagoon, schist cliffs. Less known than Amado but more visually spectacular. Access from Carrapateira via a sandy path.

Praia do Amado is beautiful winter and summer, but the wind is constant and the swell frank - hence its inclusion on the world surf circuit in winter. Surf schools operate on the beach from September to June. In summer, families replace the surfers.

activities.text: If you surf, Praia do Amado is on the WSL world circuit in winter (regular waves, sandy bottom). Surf lessons are available year-round via village schools. Off-season, count on €40-60 per 2h lesson.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Lisbon and Southern Portugal Tour in 15 Days

visits.text: Explore Algar Seco in picturesque Carvoeiro, a unique coastal area featuring remarkable caves, grottos, and limestone cliffs sculpted by rain and sea over millions of years. Boardwalks provide accessible viewing, but adventurous visitors can explore pathways and staircases carved between the rocks that feel like navigating a natural maze. The most beautiful cave features two natural windows overlooking the coast, discovered by descending stairs at the car park's end.

Hike the famous Seven Hanging Valleys Trail, the most popular coastal walk in the Algarve following breathtaking scenery between Praia da Marinha and Praia do Vale de Centeanes. This 5.7-kilometer route passes Benagil Cave, Benagil Beach, Carvalho Beach, and Alfazina Lighthouse among the highlights. The trail provides easy access to one of the most scenic stretches of coastline in all of Portugal.

Photograph Praia da Marinha, where postcard-perfect views have made this one of the most recognizable beaches in Portugal. The clifftop paths overlooking the beach reveal the typical Algarve coastline featured in guidebooks worldwide. Walking westward from the viewpoint near the parking area reveals the most spectacular scenery along this stunning stretch of coast.

activities.text: Take a boat trip to the famous Benagil Cave, the largest and best-known sea cave in the Algarve accessible only from the water. This cathedral-like cavern is so massive that several boats can enter simultaneously, with sunlight streaming through its collapsed roof. The entire coastline here is dotted with sea caves creating spectacular scenery whether explored by speedboat or kayak.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal Grand Tour: Algarve Coast to Northern Mountain Peaks

visits.text: Journey to Sintra, a UNESCO World Heritage cultural landscape where romantic palaces rise from forested hillsides. Portuguese royalty chose this misty mountain retreat for summer residences, leaving an extraordinary concentration of architectural treasures. The town's fairytale atmosphere has captivated visitors from Lord Byron to modern travelers.

Explore the Palácio Nacional de Sintra, the medieval royal palace dominating the town center with distinctive conical kitchen chimneys visible for miles. This summer residence preserves remarkable interiors including rooms covered in decorative azulejo tiles and elaborate Mudéjar ceilings. The palace represents the longest continuously inhabited royal residence in Portugal.

Ascend to the Palácio da Pena, a flamboyant 19th-century palace crowning the highest peak in an explosion of color and architectural fantasy. This romantic masterpiece combines Gothic towers, Manueline decoration, Moorish elements, and Renaissance domes in a riot of yellows, reds, and blues. The surrounding park of exotic trees extends the magical atmosphere across the mountainside.

Stand at Cabo da Roca, the westernmost point of continental Europe, where dramatic cliffs plunge into the Atlantic beneath a historic lighthouse. This windswept headland marks where the European landmass meets the ocean. The raw power of crashing waves, soaring cliffs, and endless horizon creates a profound sense of standing at the edge of the world.

activities.text: Conclude the day in Cascais, an elegant beach resort attracting aristocrats since Portuguese royalty made it their summer playground. The charming old town features cobbled pedestrian streets, whitewashed buildings, and a picturesque fishing harbor still in use. Sandy beaches and relaxed coastal atmosphere provide the perfect transition before heading north.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal Grand Tour: Algarve Coast to Northern Mountain Peaks
Portugal North Bound: Lisbon to Peneda-Gerês National Park

visits.text: Marvel at the Episcopal Palace Garden of Castelo Branco, a masterpiece of Portuguese landscape art commissioned by the Bishop of Guarda D. João de Mendonça around 1720. This Baroque garden features lakes, balconies with stone balustrades, and statues of the Kings of Portugal, with those of the Spanish-rule monarchs purposely made smaller.

Discover the Barrocal Park, a unique natural space integrated into the UNESCO Naturtejo Global Geopark. Inaugurated in 2020, this 40-hectare park allows you to appreciate a granite landscape shaped over hundreds of millions of years. Explore the Lizard Tunnel, the Bee-eater Observatory, and the suspension bridge to the São Martinho viewpoint.

Visit the Cargaleiro Museum, opened to the public in 2005, which displays a remarkable collection of paintings, ceramics, sculptures, and tapestries. Beyond the work of plastic artist Manuel Cargaleiro, native of Vila Velha de Ródão, works by national and international artists can be appreciated.

Explore the Castelo Branco Embroidery Interpretation Center, where you can watch artisans producing new pieces of this centuries-old art on their looms. Castelo Branco Embroidery is a magnificent artistic expression with unique characteristics, preserved with the same dedication as previous generations.

Visit the Francisco Tavares Proença Júnior Museum, the CCCCB - Contemporary Culture Center of Castelo Branco, and the House of Memory of the Jewish Presence. These museum spaces reveal the collective identity of Castelo Branco, preserving regional memory from archaeology to contemporary art.

Explore the Textile Museum and the Stonemason Museum, spaces dedicated to the traditional arts and crafts of the region. From embroidery to granite, these museums preserve ancestral knowledge that has long filled the collective imagination of Castelo Branco.

Discover the Olive Oil Route by visiting a traditional olive press such as Fio da Beira. The olive oil of Beira-Baixa, with olive trees planted since 1359, is now classified as PDO and has won national and international awards for its exceptional quality.

activities.text: Stroll through Castelo Branco's historic heritage: the Castle and walls, São Miguel Church, São João Cross, Clock Tower, Palace of the Viscounts of Portalegre, and Graça Convent. Climb to the São Gens Viewpoint for a panoramic view over the city.

Discover the urban art and murals of Castelo Branco, including works by Vhils and the impressive mural alluding to the French Invasions. These artistic expressions represent historical episodes, traditions, and experiences of the region's people, transforming the streets into open-air museums.

Hike the region's trails: the PR2 - Castelo Branco Schist Path of 9.6 km, the PR1 - Gardunha Route of 14.8 km, or the PR3 - Sarzedas Schist Path. Observe over 150 bird species that inhabit the district, including vultures, eagles, and black storks.

Cool off at Castelo Branco's Pool-Beach, open from June to September, or at the river beaches of Almaceda and Sesmo. Stroll through the City Park, Devesa Civic Center, and Violetas Park, green spaces ideal for leisure moments.

days_plan.itin_title: Beira Interior Road Trip, Portugal: Serra da Estrela, Schist Villages & History
Beira Interior Road Trip, Portugal: Serra da Estrela, Schist Villages & History
Portugal: Historic Villages & Serra da Estrela in 8 Days

visits.text: Built in the 14th century and identified thanks to a stone tabernacle rediscovered in the 1970s, the medieval synagogue of Castelo de Vide is one of only two pre-expulsion synagogues preserved in Portugal—the other is in Tomar. The surrounding Judiaria welcomed Jews expelled from Spain in 1492 and retains its original plan with ogival doors and mezuzah marks. Free entrance, closed Mondays.

Locals have been fetching water here continuously since the 16th century: the Fonte da Vila is a Mannerist fountain at the heart of the Judiaria, at the corner of Rua da Fonte and Rua da Judiaria. Modest, almost forgotten by tourists. That's precisely why it deserves a stop.

Climb up to Castelo de Vide Castle to see Marvão on the northeast horizon, perched on its ridge five kilometers away. The two fortresses have faced each other for centuries. Castelo de Vide's medieval castle controls the Sever valley; clear view over the Serra de São Mamede.

activities.text: The Judiaria is short: that's its whole strength. A dozen alleys are enough to get lost in the medieval Jewish quarter, one of the best preserved in Portugal. Every ogival door is a document. Pick up a map at the tourist office—the signage is insufficient to understand the houses.

If you pass through on a Saturday morning, Castelo de Vide's main square comes alive early with a local market—vegetables, cheeses, honey. Upper Alentejo villages have weekly markets that survive despite depopulation.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal Discovery Tour: From Lisbon to Alentejo Plains
Lisbon and Upper Alentejo Fortresses in 8 Days

visits.text: Two granite boars greet you at the main gate of Castelo Mendo. They are verracos—Celtic-Iberian animal sculptures associated with the Vettones, a people who inhabited the region before the Romans. Similar verracos are found throughout the western Iberian Peninsula. Allow 10 seconds to understand why they're there, 30 minutes for the rest of the village.

The walls inside Castelo Mendo date partly to the reign of Dom Sancho II (13th century), the second enclosure to Dom Dinis. Inside, Manueline houses, a medieval judiaria, and in the stones of the old Paço do Concelho, two carved figures face each other: Mendo and Menda, whose story is unknown.

activities.text: Look in the stones for the figures of Mendo and Menda—two carved faces watching each other from opposite sides of the alley, from different buildings. No one knows who they were or who had them engraved. Local tradition would have them as lovers condemned to see each other without touching.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal: Historic Villages & Serra da Estrela in 8 Days

places.name: Castelo Rodrigo

visits.text: This village faces Spain: the border is 5 km away. From the top of the promontory, the Spanish ridgeline is visible in clear weather. The political isolation explains Castelo Rodrigo's strange history—a village more loyal to Madrid than to Lisbon for sixty years.

The ruins of the Palácio de Cristóvão de Moura are the trace of a punished betrayal. Cristóvão de Moura was Philip II of Spain's chief diplomat for seizing the Portuguese throne in 1580. Named Viceroy of Portugal and Marquis of Castelo Rodrigo, he had his palace built here. In 1640, as soon as independence was restored, the people burned the building. Never rebuilt. The ruins are freely accessible.

The Manueline windows still standing on the village façades testify to Castelo Rodrigo's importance in the 16th century. The medieval cistern within the walls, whose horseshoe arch suggests 13th-century construction, is accessible and often overlooked by visitors.

activities.text: If you pass late in the day, the raking light brings out the golden stones of the burned palace. The ruins have been used for open-air concerts—the acoustics are good. The Manueline windows still standing on the façades give an idea of what the building once was.

Walk down toward the almond terraces covering the slope below the village—the blossoms in January-February are photographed by visitors from across the peninsula. Off-season, the dry-stone terraces are simply beautiful.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal: Historic Villages & Serra da Estrela in 8 Days

map_keywords: Palácio Cristóvão de Moura Castelo Rodrigo

places.name: Castro Daire

visits.text: Ascend Serra de São Macário, a mountain range offering some of the finest panoramic views in all of northern Portugal. The winding drive to the summit is an adventure itself, with narrow roads climbing through dramatic highland landscapes. At the top, sweeping vistas extend across valleys, villages, and distant mountain ranges in every direction.

Visit Aldeia da Pena, one of northern Portugal's most isolated and picturesque traditional stone villages hidden deep in a valley. Narrow cobbled streets covered with straw wind between rough-hewn stone houses that seem grown from the landscape. Goats roam freely, their bells mingling with a murmuring stream, creating an atmosphere unchanged for centuries.

activities.text: Watch sunset from the summit of Serra de São Macário, where peace and spectacular scenery create truly magical moments. The elevated viewpoint often provides complete solitude with surrounding landscape bathed in golden light. Hiking trails radiate from the summit through forests and across high moorland for further exploration.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal North Bound: Lisbon to Peneda-Gerês National Park
Portugal's Trás-os-Montes: Bragança, Douro Valley & Montesinho Park

map_keywords: Serra de São Macário hiking trails

visits.text: The steppe stretches as far as the eye can see from Castro Verde: cereals, scattered cork oaks, immense sky. This Zona de Proteção Especial is classified by BirdLife International as one of Europe's most important areas for steppe birds - little bustards, pin-tailed sandgrouse, lesser kestrels. The LPN (Liga para a Protecção da Natureza) manages several reserves accessible to the public.

The Basílica Real de Nossa Senhora da Conceição preserves an 18th-century azulejo cycle recounting the Battle of Ourique of 1139 - mythical battle of Portugal's founding, where Afonso Henriques allegedly saw Christ appear to him. The exact location of the battle remains contested by historians, but the building marks the traditional site.

activities.text: If you're here in March-April, little bustards parade at sunrise in the fields around Castro Verde. The LPN organizes guided birdwatching outings - information at the Castro Verde nature house. Binoculars essential.

days_plan.itin_title: The Grand Lisbon and Southern Portugal Tour in 15 Days

visits.text: Discover the famous Chaves Thermal Baths, whose thermal waters spring forth at over 70°C. These millennial baths, already appreciated by the Romans, are renowned for their curative properties against muscular and digestive ailments. Taste the water at Fonte do Povo, a unique sensory experience even if the taste may surprise you!

Cross the remarkable Roman Bridge of Trajan, an impressive 140-meter monument that has spanned the Tâmega River for nearly 2,000 years. This 12-arch bridge, one of the best preserved on the Iberian Peninsula, bears witness to the genius of Roman engineers and the strategic importance of Chaves on the road between Braga and Astorga.

activities.text: Stroll through the historic center of Chaves, admiring the Keep Tower of the medieval castle and the Fort of São Francisco transformed into a charming hotel. Taste the famous pastéis de Chaves, meat-filled pastries that have made the gastronomic reputation of this welcoming spa town.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal's Trás-os-Montes: Bragança, Douro Valley & Montesinho Park

visits.text: Explore the University of Coimbra, one of Europe's oldest and most prestigious academic institutions, founded in 1290. This UNESCO World Heritage Site crowns the city's hilltop, its magnificent baroque library Joanina housing over 200,000 ancient volumes beneath gilded ceilings and exotic wood shelving. The ceremonial halls and historic classrooms transport visitors through centuries of scholarly tradition.

Visit the Sé Velha, Coimbra's Romanesque Old Cathedral built in the 12th century during the tumultuous period of the Reconquista. This fortress-like church, with its crenellated walls and narrow windows, represents one of Portugal's finest examples of Romanesque architecture. Inside, the Gothic cloister and Renaissance altarpiece showcase the evolution of Portuguese religious art.

Wander through Coimbra's historic center, where narrow medieval streets wind between ancient stone buildings. The city's unique geography creates two distinct zones: the Baixa with its bustling commercial streets and the Alta where centuries of academic tradition permeate every cobblestone.

activities.text: Discover the Monastery of Santa Cruz, final resting place of Portugal's first two kings, Afonso Henriques and Sancho I. This 12th-century monastery features an ornate Manueline portal, Renaissance sacristy, and stunning azulejo panels depicting the life of Saint Augustine.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal Discovery Tour: From Lisbon to Alentejo Plains

visits.text: Discover the iconic striped houses of Costa Nova, known locally as palheiros. These charming wooden structures, painted in vibrant reds, blues, greens, and yellows, were originally fishermen's huts used to store equipment. Today they stand as one of Portugal's most photographed coastal landmarks, creating a rainbow of color against the Atlantic backdrop.

Climb the Farol da Barra, Portugal's tallest lighthouse standing at 62 meters. Built in 1893, this striking red and white tower offers panoramic views across the Atlantic Ocean and the Aveiro lagoon. The ascent rewards visitors with breathtaking vistas of the coastline stretching toward the horizon.

activities.text: Relax on the golden sands of Costa Nova do Prado beach, a favorite destination for surfers and sunbathers alike. The powerful Atlantic waves make this an excellent spot for water sports, while the long stretches of sand invite peaceful walks along the shore.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal Discovery Tour: From Lisbon to Alentejo Plains

places.name: Covao Da Ametade

visits.text: Covão da Ametade is a glacial basin at the source of the Zêzere—a granite cirque at 1500 m altitude, fed by winter snowfields. It's the birth of the river that crosses all of Beira before joining the Tagus. The water comes out cold and clear even in August.

The trail from Manteigas to Covão da Ametade follows the glacial valley for 7 km one way. The elevation gain is moderate and the path marked. At sunrise, the waters are still and the reflection of the granite wall in the lake is crisp.

activities.text: Set out on foot from Manteigas at dawn—the Zêzere glacial trail is one of the finest in the Serra. 14 km round trip, about 3h30. Hiking boots required: the trail gets rocky after the first 500 m.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal: Historic Villages & Serra da Estrela in 8 Days

map_keywords: source of Zêzere Serra da Estrela

places.name: Covao Dos Conchos

visits.text: Covão dos Conchos is an artificial spillway at the bottom of a mountain lake—a circular funnel that swallows water in silence and diverts it under the mountain to another basin. Went viral on social media for its "portal to the depths" effect. The hydraulic reality is simpler, but the visual effect delivers.

The trail between Lagoa Comprida and Covão dos Conchos runs about 5 km round trip from the Lagoa parking lot. The high moorlands of the Serra da Estrela crossed along the way are among Portugal's most open landscapes.

activities.text: Do the Lagoa Comprida - Covão dos Conchos loop early in the morning to see the spillway without the photographers. After 10 a.m. in summer, it's crowded. The morning light on the water is better anyway.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal: Historic Villages & Serra da Estrela in 8 Days

map_keywords: Covão dos Conchos Serra da Estrela

visits.text: Begin your Covilhã exploration at Santa Maria Maior Church, a stunning 16th-century Baroque edifice in the heart of the historic center. Marvel at the exquisite blue and white azulejo tiles adorning its exterior, beautifully depicting scenes from the life of the Virgin Mary, showcasing centuries of Portuguese religious artistry.

Climb to Portas do Sol Viewpoint for panoramic vistas stretching across Covilhã's rooftops to the majestic Serra da Gardunha mountain range. This elevated terrace features a historic sundial and several remarkable street art murals from the WOOL Urban Art Festival.

Explore Praça do Município, the vibrant heart of Covilhã where the impressive Town Hall, ornate Misericórdia Church, and elegant Municipal Theatre surround a statue honoring Pêro da Covilhã, the legendary 15th-century explorer sent by King John II to discover the spice routes of India and Ethiopia.

Visit the Wool Museum documenting Covilhã's heritage as one of Portugal's main wool producers. Housed in the historic Real Fábrica de Panos and Real Fábrica Veiga, the museum showcases the traditional textile processes that once powered hundreds of local factories.

Journey to Varanda dos Carqueijais viewpoint, just 10 minutes from Covilhã, offering one of Serra da Estrela's most spectacular panoramas. The distinctive metal viewing platform has become an iconic photography spot, framing the dramatic mountain landscape in a unique architectural gesture.

Ascend to Torre, mainland Portugal's highest point at nearly 2,000 meters elevation. This dramatic summit, just 30 minutes from Covilhã, attracts visitors year-round seeking snow in winter and cool mountain air in summer. Portugal's only natural ski resort operates here during the coldest months.

Discover Covão d'Ametade, one of Serra da Estrela's most enchanting glacial valleys. This fairy-tale landscape of dramatic rock formations, crystal-clear streams, and alpine meadows captivates visitors in every season, whether blanketed in snow or carpeted with wildflowers.

activities.text: Embark on the WOOL Urban Art Route, an extraordinary open-air museum featuring works by international artists including Bordalo II's iconic recycled material owl, Third's massive heart mural, and Daniel Eime's intricate portraits. This annual festival has transformed neglected walls into vibrant canvases throughout the historic center.

Cool off at Unhais da Serra river beach, a pristine natural swimming spot surrounded by lush mountain scenery. The well-maintained facilities and crystal-clear waters make this an ideal escape from summer heat, just 30 minutes from Covilhã.

days_plan.itin_title: Portugal Discovery Tour: From Lisbon to Alentejo Plains
Portugal Discovery Tour: From Lisbon to Alentejo Plains

visits.text: Almost nothing: an arch and three granite pillars. The Varanda do Grão-Prior is all that remains of the palace of the Knights of the Order of Malta in Crato. A town ex-libris, as locals say. It's from this loggia that the prior asserted his power over the Priorado, a vast territory that extended over a good part of central Portugal.

Dom Manuel I married Eleanor of Austria here in 1518: Crato's Praça do Município has seen kings pass through. Today it's a quiet provincial square, with the municipal museum in a baroque palace, the symbol of the Maltese Cross carved everywhere, and cats on the windowsills.

activities.text: Have a glass of tinto alentejano in Crato's praça—Portalegre wines have body and an altitude freshness rare in Alentejo. The town is sleepy but the square's bars serve well. A well-deserved break before Flor da Rosa.

days_plan.itin_title: Lisbon and Upper Alentejo Fortresses in 8 Days

visits.text: The Cromeleque dos Almendres is 15 km from Évora, but this megalithic circle of 95 granite monoliths—dating back about 6,000 years—is one of the best-preserved megalithic ensembles on the Iberian Peninsula. Dirt road for the last 2 kilometers. Free visit, always deserted on weekdays before 10 a.m.

500 meters from the cromlech, the menhir dos Almendres stands alone among the olive trees—a standing stone over 2 meters tall, older than the circle itself. The path between the two is walkable in 10 minutes.

activities.text: You can combine the megalithic visit with a short walk through the montado—the cork oak forest typical of the Alentejo. The estate around the cromlech is open, with abundant wildlife in spring.

days_plan.itin_title: Border Alentejo: Guadiana and Alqueva in 7 Days

places.name: Elvas

visits.text: Marvel at the grand Amoreira Aqueduct, stretching 7 km with 843 arches, built by architect Francisco de Arruda, the same author of the Belém Tower. This impressive monument marks the entrance to the world's largest bastion fortification, classified as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Explore the fortifications of Elvas, whose star-shaped defensive structures with a perimeter of nearly 10 km are a unique testimony to the evolution of military strategy up to the 19th century. The complex includes Islamic and medieval walls, the 17th-century wall belt, and the Forts of Santa Luzia and Graça.

Visit the former Cathedral, now the Church of Nossa Senhora da Assunção, the Church of the Dominicans with its original octagonal floor plan, the Manueline pillory, and the Fernandina Tower. In the streets of the historic center, identify the arches that mark the ancient entrances through the walls.

Discover Praça da República, the city's living room in Portuguese cobblestone, spacious and airy, with welcoming terraces. This is the heart of Elvas, where residents and visitors meet to enjoy the tranquil atmosphere of this border garrison town.

activities.text: Visit the museums of Elvas: the Contemporary Art Museum, the João Carpinteiro Photography Museum with cameras from 1898 and prints dated 1860, the Sacred Art Museum, and two military-themed museum spaces.

Explore the Church of São Domingos, the Third Order of São Francisco Church, and outside the walls, the Sanctuary of Senhor Jesus da Piedade where the famous annual pilgrimage takes place in September, coinciding with the São Mateus Fair.

days_plan.itin_title: Alentejo UNESCO Heritage: Évora, Elvas and Medieval Villages
Inland Alentejo to the Wild Vicentine Coast

map_keywords: Nossa Senhora da Assunção Church Elvas

places.name: Estremoz

visits.text: Visit the Medieval Castle of Estremoz from the 13th century, now transformed into the Pousada Rainha Santa Isabel. The Tower of the Three Crowns, so called because three kings participated in its construction (Afonso III, Sancho II, and Dinis), offers splendid views over the marble region.

Explore the Professor Joaquim Vermelho Municipal Museum, located opposite the keep, with exhibitions on Alentejo life and the region's famous ceramics. Highlights include the Moringues, traditional globe-shaped jugs from Estremoz.
Admire the elegant Church of Santa Maria, a 16th-century hall church in late Renaissance style that replaced a medieval temple. This monument impresses with its monumentality and decorative richness.

Visit the Chapel of Senhor Jesus dos Inocentes from the 18th century, next to the statue of Queen Saint Isabel. This pays tribute to the wife of King Dinis, famous for her charity and the miracle of the roses, who died in Estremoz.

activities.text: Stroll through Rossio Marquês de Pombal, the main square dominated by the twin marble towers of the former 17th-century Convent of the Congregados. Climb the tower for glorious views over Estremoz and visit the Sacred Art Museum.

Discover the Living Science Center in the former 15th-century Convent of the Maltese Sisters, with its beautiful cloister and exhibitions on Earth and Geology. The T-Rex skeleton replica is a must-see attraction for all ages.

Explore the Porta de Santarém, the main entrance to the medieval village enclosed by the 13th-century wall, and the 17th-century Porta dos Currais. Also admire the Manueline pillory and Lago do Gadanha with its iconic statue.

days_plan.itin_title: Alentejo UNESCO Heritage: Évora, Elvas and Medieval Villages
Inland Alentejo to the Wild Vicentine Coast
Border Alentejo: Guadiana and Alqueva in 7 Days

map_keywords: Medieval Castle Estremoz Tower of Three Crowns

places.name: Evora

visits.text: Discover Praça do Giraldo, the beating heart of Évora, with its iconic arcades, the Church of Santo Antão, and the historic fountain that served for centuries as one of the main water supply structures for the population. The square's name pays tribute to Geraldo Sem Pavor, a semi-legendary figure from the city's reconquest from the Moors.

Marvel at the Roman Temple of Évora, dating from the 1st century, the main vestige from when Ebora Liberalitas Iulia was one of the most notable Roman cities in Lusitania. Contrary to popular belief, this temple was likely dedicated to the imperial cult rather than to the goddess Diana.

Visit the imposing Évora Cathedral, the largest medieval cathedral in Portugal, built between the 12th and 13th centuries. Don't miss the cloisters, the main chapel, the Sacred Art Museum, and climb to the rooftop for a breathtaking panoramic view over this UNESCO World Heritage city.

Enter the Church of São Francisco, built in Manueline-Mudéjar and Renaissance style in the 15th century, which once had the Royal Palace annexed and was intimately connected to Portugal's maritime expansion era. Inside, the famous Chapel of Bones will remain forever etched in your memory.

Visit the College of the Holy Spirit, the main building of the University of Évora founded by Cardinal Dom Henrique in the 16th century. The grand cloister is a must-see, bearing witness to the historical importance of this centuries-old educational institution.

Discover the Largo and Fountain of Portas de Moura, one of the most emblematic squares of the 16th-century city, surrounded by noble mansions of the most important families of the era. This square preserves the aristocratic atmosphere of Renaissance Évora.

Admire the Renaissance facade of the Church of Graça from the 16th century, a beautiful architectural example located in Largo da Graça. This temple demonstrates the wealth of Évora's religious heritage that extends throughout the city.

Visit the Art and Culture Center, the former Palace of the Inquisition, now a space of the Eugénio de Almeida Foundation dedicated to arts and culture with a special focus on contemporary art. A fascinating contrast between the building's dark history and its current cultural vocation.

Discover the Almendres Cromlech, built nearly 7,000 years ago, the largest megalithic enclosure on the Iberian Peninsula and one of the oldest in the world. This prehistoric monument testifies to human occupation of this region since time immemorial.

Just over 1 km from the cromlech, visit the Almendres Menhir, another impressive megalithic monument that complements this extraordinary archaeological complex. These monuments are unique testimonies to the civilizations that inhabited the Alentejo thousands of years ago.

activities.text: Explore Largo Conde Vila Flor, home to some of the city's main buildings: the stunning Church of the Lóios with its blue and white azulejo tiles at their finest, the 15th-century Convent of the Lóios now a pousada, the Dukes of Cadaval Palace, and the Évora Museum with nearly 20,000 objects.

Stroll through the magnificent Public Garden built in the 19th century, where you'll find the 15th-century Palace of Dom Manuel, the place where Vasco da Gama was appointed commander of the fleet that would discover the maritime route to India. Don't miss the charming Fake Ruins with their peacocks.

Discover the imposing Walls of Évora surrounding the entire historic center and the grand Silver Water Aqueduct, inaugurated in the 16th century to supply the city. Look for the houses built between its arches, a secret that not all visitors discover.

Climb to the Windmills of Alto de São Bento for the best panoramic view over Évora. This viewpoint offers a unique perspective of the World Heritage city, with its monuments and walls silhouetted against the Alentejo plain.

Visit the Cartuxa Winery, one of the most prestigious in Portugal, where you can taste the renowned Alentejo wine and olive oil. This wine tourism experience is essential for those wishing to discover the authentic flavors of the region.

Travel the Évora Ecovia - Mora Branch on foot or by bicycle, an alternative way to discover the Alentejo landscape. This route allows direct contact with nature and the golden plains that characterize this region.
